title: Evangelion Intimacy
wikitext:
[]: Evangelion Intimacy             Quote Post by Tribblepoo » Thu Sep 13, 2012 6:22 pm First off, please forgive the slightly misleading title. I am a sucker for word play that lends itself to absolutely foul mental images. Anyway, this thread... http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/13171/Asuka-and-rape/ ...led me along an interesting line of thought. Each of the major characters in the series seems to be an analog for the complications inherent in certain forms of intimacy, or the lack of that sort of intimacy. Using the above example; Asuka represents sexual intimacy and the violation thereof. I also mentioned Ritsuko in that thread, who seems to be a representation of the flip-side of sexual intimacy: non-platonic emotional intimacy. Other characters seem to follow similar, if somewhat highly specialized, lines. Misato could represent the intimate relationship between parent and child, Fuyutski unrequited love and desire for intimacy with one specific person, Gendo would be a love lost (the loss of intimacy) and Rei the intimacy blank slate, never having known true intimacy of any sort. Shinji is, in a way, all of these rolled up into one. I haven't been able to really pin down what the roles of characters such as Kaji, Toji and Kensuke, Hikari or even Pen-Pen have in this, but I have a feeling that they do play some role in the whole intimacy scheme. For instance, my initial thoughts were that Toji represented the friendship aspect of intimacy, but that seems to actually fit Hikari more, so I am unsure.

[]:             Quote Post by JoeD80 » Thu Sep 13, 2012 7:14 pm Human connection and sexualty certainly are themes that are central to the series, but I don't think it can be broken down into those kind of individual pieces like "character A represents true love", "character B represents surface love". I think the characters are more multi-faceted than that; e.g. Misato has a nurturing side and an impulsive side too.

[]:             Quote Post by riffraff11235 » Thu Sep 13, 2012 7:19 pm Kensuke could be the obsessive aspect of intimacy. He is a military otaku, and he's obsessed with the prospect of becoming an Eva-pilot for the better part of the series. Hikari and Touji could be an example of young love. That's the best I've got for them.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Thu Sep 13, 2012 8:13 pm Narratively? Evangelion is Anti-Intimacy. Every character is stuck on his or her little existential island, trying to grasp at someone who's just out of reach. That, friends, is what they call drama. It's not exactly a wild trope. Soap Operas and Teen Dramas pull of the same shit to slightly lesser degrees. Cinematographically? You have Anno playing with distance and composition which really drives the point home and makes it unique.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Fri Sep 14, 2012 9:18 am I think instead of Shinji being the compilation or summary of all the intimacy aspects, i would set the NGE series itself as the compilation, either that or one of the more divine/plot-vital characters (Yui, Lilith, SEELE, Instrumentality, etc.) as the one to be the analogue for intimacy in general, actually, i think the Instrumentality project is perfect for the summary. Shinji's analogue, i think would be more of a sort of platonic intimacy, the nature of intimacy in itself, or the relation between the self and intimacy (the last one due to Shinji's self-centered -almost absorved- nature). Well, more accurately, a sort of pure or unambiguous intimacy that wouldn't exactly fit on terms of friendship, romantic or sexual sense. An intimacy of comfort, approval or support. More of a non-romantic way of a life partner, if that makes any sense. Simply put, Shinji would be more questioning of whether or not people loved him, truly/honestly loved him, and what "love" or "intimacy" means in the first place. On this premise i think Kaji's analogue would fit in the sense of false intimacy, or voluntary/involuntary "masks" if that makes any sense. He lies and pretends towards many people, and above all to himself, while being bitterly aware of that. Pen-pen doesn't fit in this because he's a penguin. And i think both Toji and Hikari represent aspects of friendship, instead of just one of them. Cinematographically? You have Anno playing with distance and composition which really drives the point home and makes it unique.
